key_insight: Moving-window inverse modeling shows that hydrogels do not behave like constant-parameter materials during inertial cavitation; the apparent shear modulus and viscosity evolve over time, revealing where the constitutive assumption breaks down.
engineering_relevance: This provides a diagnostic tool for high-rate soft-matter characterization and helps guide better constitutive models for bubble–material interactions in biomedical and soft robotics applications.
method: Laser-induced cavitation experiments coupled with bubble-dynamics simulation
Time-resolved parameter estimation using a modified iterative ensemble Kalman smoother with multiple data assimilation (MIEnKS-MDA)
Fit a neo-Hookean Kelvin–Voigt model over overlapping moving windows to expose window dependence
evidence_signal: Across polyacrylamide and gelatin hydrogels, inferred modulus/viscosity changed during cavitation, generally decreasing then plateauing in PAAm, with strong temperature-dependent trends in gelatin and notable variation during the first two bubble collapses.
watch_item: The results remain tied to the prescribed NHKV model and windowing strategy, so the inferred parameters are diagnostic rather than universal material constants.
paper_title: Limits of constant-parameter constitutive models for hydrogels under inertial cavitation
